description: Rotary breech mechanism with Geneva drive and caseless ammunition. Most mechanically complex infantry weapon ever produced.

bones.name: Outer Shell
bones.description: Outer polymer housing — what the shooter holds. All internal mechanisms float inside this shell on guide rails
bones.placement: Place at the center of the outer housing, near the pistol grip

bones.name: Floating Assembly
bones.description: Barrel + breech + feed mechanism slide backward inside outer shell on guide rails. In burst mode, fires 3 rounds before this assembly hits the rear buffer — shooter feels recoil only after the 3rd round
bones.placement: Place at the front of the internal mechanism, centered in the outer shell bore axis

bones.name: Barrel
bones.description: Fixed relative to the floating assembly. Moves with it during recoil but has no independent motion
bones.placement: Place at the chamber end of the barrel, centered on bore axis

bones.name: Breech Cylinder
bones.description: Rotating cylinder with a chamber bored through it. Rotates exactly 90 degrees per step via a 4-slot Geneva mechanism. Positions: feed (0), fire (90), extract (180), clear (270). The Geneva drive creates characteristic snap-dwell motion — rapid rotation then dead stop
bones.placement: Place at the center of the breech cylinder, on the rotation axis perpendicular to the barrel

bones.name: Chamber Insert
bones.description: Replaceable chamber piece inside the breech cylinder. Rotates with cylinder. Removable for maintenance animation
bones.placement: Place inside the breech cylinder bore, at the chamber position

bones.name: Gas Piston
bones.description: Gas piston driven rearward by propellant gas. Its linear stroke is converted to rotary motion of the breech cylinder through the gear train (connecting rod → spur gear → actuating gear)
bones.placement: Place at the gas piston head, above or beside the barrel

bones.name: Connecting Rod
bones.description: Links gas piston linear motion to the spur gear. Pivots approximately 30 degrees each direction as piston reciprocates
bones.placement: Place at the pivot point connecting the gas piston to the spur gear

bones.name: Spur Gear

drivers.description: Spur gear rotates as gas piston reciprocates. Gear ratio converts small piston stroke to larger angular displacement

bones.description: Intermediate gear in the drive train. Converts connecting rod oscillation to continuous rotation. Meshes with the actuating gear
bones.placement: Place at the spur gear axle, between the connecting rod and the actuating gear

bones.name: Actuating Gear
bones.description: Final gear that meshes with notches on the breech cylinder base. Drives the Geneva mechanism — its pin engages the cylinder's slots to produce 90-degree snap rotations
bones.placement: Place at the actuating gear axle, adjacent to the breech cylinder base

bones.name: Striker
bones.description: Fires the caseless round when breech cylinder is aligned with barrel (90-degree position). Released by striker spring when cam allows
bones.placement: Place at the rear of the breech, on the bore axis behind the cylinder

bones.name: Cocking Handle
bones.description: Unique rotary cocking handle — rotates 360 degrees counterclockwise to charge the weapon. Unlike any other firearm's charging mechanism. Mounted on the outer shell (does not reciprocate)
bones.placement: Place at the cocking handle axle on the outer shell, typically on the left side

bones.name: Toothed Wheel
bones.description: Gear wheel driven by the cocking handle rotation. Transmits cocking force to the internal mechanism
bones.placement: Place on the same axle as the cocking handle, inside the housing

bones.name: Magazine Follower
bones.description: Pushes caseless rounds from the top-mounted magazine down into the breech cylinder feed position. Magazine sits horizontally on top of the weapon
bones.placement: Place at the top of the magazine feed area, where rounds enter the cylinder

bones.name: Trigger
bones.description: Standard trigger pull releases sear mechanism. Connected to outer shell (not floating assembly) so trigger feel is consistent regardless of assembly position
bones.placement: Place at the trigger pin in the pistol grip area

bones.name: Selector
bones.description: 4-position selector: Safe, Semi, 3-round Burst (2100 RPM), Full Auto (460 RPM). Burst mode is the G11's signature feature — three rounds fired before the shooter feels any recoil
bones.placement: Place at the selector lever on the left side of the housing

burst_recoil_behavior.description: In burst mode, floating assembly travels backward firing 3 rounds before hitting buffer. User feels recoil only after 3rd round

physics.source: Patent US3997994, Armourers Bench reference photos, HK factory documentation
